assessment_items.question_content:
**What historical artistic tradition does Kara Walker use to create the figures in *Darkytown Rebellion*? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First used as a courtly art form in 16th century Europe, silhouette cut-out allow Walker to explore the visual cues that contribute to racial stereotyping. 

**[Read this text to review.](/global-art-architecture/a/kara-walker-darkytown-rebellion)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which source was the basis of Kara Walker's *Darkytown Rebellion*? **

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4fa10f47b2c628869db19ee89a363094.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: *The Daily Constitution 1878* (2011) emerged after Walker read an 1878 article from an Atlanta newspaper about a mob lynching a black woman.

Walker appropriated figures from a painting called *Darkytown*  by an unschooled artist she discovered, adding additional racial stereotypes and reworking the scene to be one of rebellion.


**[Read this text to review.](/global-art-architecture/a/kara-walker-darkytown-rebellion)**

choices.content: Multi media light shows of the 1960s
A newspaper article about a woman who was lynched
A landscape painting by an unschooled artist
A documentary film about a slave uprising

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which best describes Walker's motivations in creating images that recall African American stereotypes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Walker does not attempt to correct a late-nineteenth century depictions of African-Americans, but asks about the power of such images today. 

**[Read this text to review.](/global-art-architecture/a/kara-walker-darkytown-rebellion)**

choices.content: She corrects past accounts of African American experience.
She asks questions about the power of such images today.
She documents historical events in museum settings.
She erases atrocities of the past as a way to improve race relations today.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which is NOT a component of Kara Walker's *Darkytown Rebellion* ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The color projections, whose abstract shapes recall the 1960s liquid light shows projected with psychedelic music, heighten the surreality of *Darkytown Rebellion*.

Although the Aunt Jemima logo is not included in *Darkytown Rebellion,*  Walker's work reflects on such historical representations of African Americans in American visual culture. 

**[Read this text to review.](/global-art-architecture/a/kara-walker-darkytown-rebellion)**

choices.content: a figure with a severed limb
a woman with a bonnet and hoop skirt
bright light projections in abstract forms
